citation_short: Wang et al., ChestX-ray8
task: Thoracic disease classification and weakly supervised localization
key_findings: Introduced a hospital-scale chest X-ray benchmark and showed that common thoracic diseases can be detected and weakly localized from image-level labels.
strengths: Foundational dataset paper; defines the benchmark that motivated later chest X-ray studies.
limitations: Labels are text-mined and noisy; early benchmark results leave large room for improvement.
relevance_to_capstone: Dataset foundation for the thesis and for Chapter 2 background.
notes: Use in Section 2.1 when introducing public chest X-ray benchmarks.

citation_short: Rajpurkar et al., CheXNet
key_findings: Demonstrated strong pneumonia detection on chest X-rays using a DenseNet121 model initialized with pretrained weights.
strengths: Highly influential chest X-ray paper; directly relevant because it popularized DenseNet121 in this domain.
limitations: Focuses on pneumonia rather than a general normal-vs-abnormal task; interpretability is not the main emphasis.
relevance_to_capstone: Strong baseline reference for DenseNet121 and transfer learning discussion.
notes: Use in Sections 2.1 and 2.2.

citation_short: He et al., ResNet
key_findings: Introduced residual learning, enabling successful optimization of very deep convolutional networks.
strengths: Foundational architecture paper; explains why ResNet50 is a justified baseline.
limitations: Not a medical imaging paper; results come from natural images rather than clinical data.
relevance_to_capstone: Provides theoretical and architectural basis for selecting ResNet50.
notes: Use in Section 2.2 as model background.

citation_short: Huang et al., DenseNet
key_findings: Proposed dense connectivity for feature reuse and improved gradient flow across deep networks.
strengths: Foundational architecture paper; supports why DenseNet121 is effective in image classification.
limitations: Not specific to medical imaging and does not address chest X-ray data directly.
relevance_to_capstone: Provides theoretical and architectural basis for selecting DenseNet121.
notes: Use in Section 2.2 as model background.

citation_short: Tajbakhsh et al., Full Training or Fine Tuning
key_findings: Showed that transfer learning can be highly effective for medical imaging tasks, especially when labeled data are limited.
strengths: Directly supports the core research question comparing scratch training and transfer learning.
limitations: Not specific to chest X-rays; results vary by modality and task.
relevance_to_capstone: Primary justification for transfer learning experiments in Chapter 3.
notes: Use in Section 2.3.

citation_short: Irvin et al., CheXpert
task: Chest radiograph classification with uncertainty labels
key_findings: Introduced a large chest radiograph dataset with uncertainty-aware label handling and expert comparison.
strengths: Important benchmark paper that clarifies dataset design and label uncertainty issues.
limitations: Dataset is different from NIH ChestX-ray14, so direct numerical comparisons with the thesis should be cautious.
relevance_to_capstone: Useful benchmark context and discussion of uncertainty in chest X-ray labels.
notes: Use in Section 2.1 as benchmark context.

citation_short: Selvaraju et al., Grad-CAM
key_findings: Proposed Grad-CAM as a practical explanation method for highlighting class-relevant image regions.
strengths: Main explainability method used in the thesis; easy to transfer into chest X-ray analysis.
limitations: Heatmaps provide supportive evidence rather than causal proof of model reasoning.
relevance_to_capstone: Core reference for Section 2.4 and later Grad-CAM methodology.
notes: Use in Chapters 2 and 3.

citation_short: Litjens et al., Survey on Deep Learning in Medical Image Analysis
key_findings: Summarized major deep learning applications in medical image analysis and identified common opportunities and challenges.
strengths: Strong general background source for medical image analysis and transfer learning context.
limitations: Published before many recent chest X-ray benchmark papers; not chest-X-ray-specific.
relevance_to_capstone: Useful for Chapter 2 background and positioning.
notes: Use in introduction to Chapter 2 and Section 2.3.

citation_short: Shin et al., Deep CNN for Computer-Aided Detection
dataset: Multiple radiology datasets including chest X-ray / CT
key_findings: Compared CNN architectures and showed the practical value of transfer learning for medical image tasks.
strengths: Important early evidence that transfer learning helps in radiology applications.
limitations: Not focused on a single chest X-ray benchmark and predates later large-scale datasets.
relevance_to_capstone: Historical context for Section 2.3.
notes: Use when discussing early medical transfer learning evidence.

citation_short: Ghassemi et al., Opportunities in Machine Learning for Healthcare
metrics_reported: Conceptual overview rather than benchmark metrics
key_findings: Discussed broad opportunities and challenges in applying machine learning to healthcare, including deployment and trust concerns.
strengths: Useful for framing why performance alone is insufficient in healthcare research.
limitations: Not a chest X-ray paper and not centered on specific imaging experiments.
relevance_to_capstone: Supports high-level motivation for trustworthy and clinically meaningful AI.
notes: Use selectively in Introduction or Discussion rather than as a core imaging source.

citation_short: Baltruschat et al., Multi-Label Chest X-Ray Classification
model_or_method: ResNet-50 variants with and without transfer learning; class activation maps
key_findings: Systematically compared scratch training, transfer learning, fine-tuning, and architecture variants on ChestX-ray14.
strengths: Very close to the thesis because it explicitly compares transfer learning and architecture choices on the same dataset family.
limitations: Focuses on multi-label prediction and ResNet-centered comparisons rather than a simpler binary task.
relevance_to_capstone: Direct benchmark comparison and one of the most relevant prior studies.
notes: Use in Sections 2.1, 2.2, and 2.3.

citation_short: Pan and Yang, Survey on Transfer Learning
metrics_reported: Conceptual taxonomy rather than benchmark metrics
key_findings: Defined major transfer learning settings and established the conceptual vocabulary used across later studies.
strengths: Foundational theory source for discussing transfer learning clearly and correctly.
limitations: Not medical imaging specific and predates deep CNN-based transfer learning practice.
relevance_to_capstone: Provides theoretical foundation for Section 2.3.
notes: Use to define transfer learning before medical examples.

citation_short: Yao et al., Learning to Diagnose from Scratch
key_findings: Showed that competitive chest X-ray classification can be achieved without relying on pretrained weights by modeling label dependencies.
strengths: Important counterpoint to transfer learning; supports why scratch training should still be tested.
limitations: Focused on multi-label dependency modeling rather than the thesis binary setup.
relevance_to_capstone: Useful for framing the scratch-vs-transfer comparison as an open empirical question.
notes: Use in Section 2.3 as a scratch-training reference.

citation_short: Hashmi et al., Efficient Pneumonia Detection Using Deep Transfer Learning
key_findings: Reported effective pneumonia detection using transfer learning and a practical deep learning workflow.
strengths: Good practical example of transfer learning in a binary chest X-ray setting.
limitations: Dataset and label setting differ from NIH ChestXray14 normal-vs-abnormal classification.
relevance_to_capstone: Supports the applied side of transfer learning in Section 2.3.
notes: Use as an applied binary-classification reference.

citation_short: Li et al., Thoracic Disease Identification and Localization With Limited Supervision
model_or_method: Unified diagnosis network with limited location supervision
key_findings: Showed that limited location annotations can improve both classification and localization in a unified model.
strengths: Relevant for discussing the broader chest X-ray literature beyond pure classification.
limitations: More complex than the current capstone scope and depends on localization supervision.
relevance_to_capstone: Good context for explaining what is intentionally excluded from the thesis scope.
notes: Use in Section 2.1 when contrasting classification with localization work.

citation_short: Calli et al., Deep Learning for Chest X-ray Analysis: A Survey
metrics_reported: Survey synthesis across tasks and datasets
key_findings: Provided a comprehensive survey of chest X-ray tasks, datasets, model families, and open challenges.
strengths: Directly useful for building Chapter 2 because it synthesizes exactly the literature family the thesis belongs to.
limitations: Survey paper, so it does not itself provide a controlled experimental result for the thesis task.
relevance_to_capstone: Excellent overview source and one of the most useful papers for Chapter 2.
notes: Use across Sections 2.1 to 2.5.

citation_short: Meedeniya et al., Chest X-ray Analysis Empowered with Deep Learning: A Systematic Review
metrics_reported: Systematic review of trends, datasets, and challenges
key_findings: Reviewed recent chest X-ray studies, especially pneumonia and COVID-19 detection, and summarized trends and challenges.
strengths: Useful as a systematic review to support claims about common methods and recurring challenges.
limitations: Leans strongly toward pneumonia and COVID-19 literature, so it is broader than the thesis focus.
relevance_to_capstone: Supports Section 2.1 and helps justify current research trends.
notes: Use as secondary survey support.

citation_short: Chaddad et al., Survey of Explainable AI Techniques in Healthcare
metrics_reported: Survey synthesis rather than benchmark metrics
key_findings: Surveyed explanation approaches in healthcare and emphasized the importance of trust, transparency, and careful interpretation.
strengths: Good high-level support for why explainability matters in healthcare AI.
limitations: Not specific to chest radiography and does not validate Grad-CAM specifically on the thesis task.
relevance_to_capstone: Supports Section 2.4 and later Discussion chapter.
notes: Use for healthcare-level motivation of explainability.

citation_short: Bhati et al., XAI Techniques for Visualizing Deep Learning Models in Medical Imaging
metrics_reported: Survey synthesis rather than benchmark metrics
key_findings: Focused on explainability techniques for visualizing deep learning behavior in medical imaging tasks.
strengths: Useful for framing Grad-CAM within a broader family of medical imaging XAI methods.
limitations: Recent survey; more methodological overview than task-specific evidence.
relevance_to_capstone: Supports Section 2.4 when comparing Grad-CAM with other visualization methods.
notes: Use as a recent XAI survey source.

citation_short: Tiu et al., Expert-level Detection via Self-Supervised Learning
key_findings: Showed that self-supervised pretraining can produce strong chest X-ray performance with reduced reliance on manual labels.
strengths: Important modern benchmark showing that representation learning matters in chest X-ray analysis.
limitations: More advanced than the current capstone scope and centered on self-supervision rather than standard transfer learning.
relevance_to_capstone: Useful as future-work and advanced benchmark context.
notes: Use briefly in Section 2.1 or Discussion as forward-looking context.
